You are an expert researcher and strategic planner with a deep understanding of experimental design and reproducibility in scientific research. 
You will receive a research paper in JSON format. 
Your task is to create a detailed and efficient plan to reproduce the experiments and methodologies described in the paper.
This plan should align precisely with the paper's methodology, experimental setup, and evaluation metrics. 

Instructions:

1. Align with the Paper: Your plan must strictly follow the methods, datasets, model configurations, hyperparameters, and experimental setups described in the paper.
2. Be Clear and Structured: Present the plan in a well-organized and easy-to-follow format, breaking it down into actionable steps.
3. Prioritize Efficiency: Optimize the plan for clarity and practical implementation while ensuring fidelity to the original experiments.

{'paper_id': 'GDEM', 'title': 'Graph Distillation with Eigenbasis Matching', 'abstract': 'The increasing amount of graph data places requirements on the efficient training of graph neural networks (GNNs). The emerging graph distillation (GD) tackles this challenge by distilling a small synthetic graph to replace the real large graph, ensuring GNNs trained on real and synthetic graphs exhibit comparable performance. However, existing methods rely on GNN-related information as supervision, including gradients, representations, and trajectories, which have two limitations. First, GNNs can affect the spectrum (i.e., eigenvalues) of the real graph, causing spectrum bias in the synthetic graph. Second, the variety of GNN architectures leads to the creation of different synthetic graphs, requiring traversal to obtain optimal performance. To tackle these issues, we propose Graph Distillation with Eigenbasis Matching (GDEM), which aligns the eigenbasis and node features of real and synthetic graphs. Meanwhile, it directly replicates the spectrum of the real graph and thus prevents the influence of GNNs. Moreover, we design a discrimination constraint to balance the effectiveness and generalization of GDEM. Theoretically, the synthetic graphs distilled by GDEM are restricted spectral approximations of the real graphs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GDEM outperforms state-of-the-art GD methods with powerful crossarchitecture generalization ability and significant distillation efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/liuyang-tian/GDEM.', 'pdf_parse': {'paper_id': 'GDEM', 'abstract': [{'text': 'The increasing amount of graph data places requirements on the efficient training of graph neural networks (GNNs). First, GNNs can affect the spectrum (i.e., eigenvalues) of the real graph, causing spectrum bias in the synthetic graph. Second, the variety of GNN architectures leads to the creation of different synthetic graphs, requiring traversal to obtain optimal performance. To tackle these issues, we propose Graph Distillation with Eigenbasis Matching (GDEM), which aligns the eigenbasis and node features of real and synthetic graphs. Meanwhile, it directly replicates the spectrum of the real graph and thus prevents the influence of GNNs. Moreover, we design a discrimination constraint to balance the effectiveness and generalization of GDEM. Theoretically, the synthetic graphs distilled by GDEM are restricted spectral approximations of the real graphs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GDEM outperforms state-of-the-art GD methods with powerful crossarchitecture generalization ability and significant distillation efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/liuyang-tian/GDEM.', 'section': 'Abstract', 'sec_num': None}], 'body_text': [{'text': 'Graph neural networks (GNNs) are proven effective in a variety of graph-related tasks (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Velickovic et al., 2018) . However, the non-Euclidean nature of graph structure presents challenges to the efficiency and scalability of GNNs (Hamilton et al., 2017) . To accelerate training, one data-centric approach is to summarize the large-scale graph into a much smaller one. Traditional methods primarily involve sparsification (Spielman & Srivastava, 2011; Yu et al., 2022) and coarsening (Loukas, 2019; Kumar et al., 2023) . However, these methods are typically designed to optimize some heuristic metrics, e.g., spectral similarity (Loukas, 2019) and pair-wise distance (Ahmed et al., 2020) , which may be irrelevant to downstream tasks, leading to sub-optimal performance.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'Recently, graph distillation (GD), a.k.a., graph condensation, has attracted considerable attention in graph reduction due to its remarkable compression ratio and lossless performance (Gao et al., 2024) . Generally, GD aims to synthesize a small graph wherein GNNs trained on it exhibit comparable performance to those trained on the real large graph. To this end, existing methods are designed to optimize the synthetic graphs by matching some GNN-related information, such as gradients (Jin et al., 2022b; a) , representations (Liu et al., 2022a) , and training trajectories (Zheng et al., 2023) , between the real and synthetic graphs. As a result, the synthetic graph aligns its distribution with the real graph and also incorporates information from downstream tasks.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'Despite the considerable progress, existing GD methods require pre-selecting a specific GNN as the distillation model, introducing two limitations: (1) GNNs used for distillation affect the real spectrum, leading to spectrum bias in the synthetic graph, i.e., a few eigenvalues dominate the data distribution. Figure 1 illustrates the total variation (TV) (Gutman & Zhou, 2006) of the real and synthetic graphs. Notably, TV reflects the smoothness of the signal over a graph. A small value of TV indicates a low-frequency distribution, and vice versa. We can observe that the values of TV in the synthetic graph distilled by a low-pass filter consistently appear lower than those in the real graph, while the opposite holds for the high-pass filter, thus verifying the existence of spectrum bias (See Section 3 for a theoretical analysis). (2) The optimal performance is obtained by traversing various GNN architectures, resulting in non-negligible computational costs. Table 1 presents the cross-architecture results of GCOND (Jin et al., 2022b) across six well-known GNNs, including GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017) , SGC (Wu et al., 2019) , PPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019) , ChebyNet (Def- PPNP 72.70 70.40 77.46 73.38 70.56 74.02 Cheb. 73.60 70.62 75.10 77.30 77.62 78.10 Bern. 67.68 73.76 74.30 77.20 78.12 78.28 GPR. 76.04 72.20 77.94 75.92 77.12 77.96 ferrard et al., 2016) , BernNet (He et al., 2021) , and GPR-GNN (Chien et al., 2021) . It can be seen that the evaluation performance of different GNNs varies greatly. As a result, existing GD methods need to distill and traverse various GNN architectures to obtain optimal performance, which significantly improves the time overhead. See Appendix A.1 for the definition of TV and Appendix A.2 for more experimental details.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'Once the weaknesses of existing methods are identified, it is natural to ask: How to distill graphs without being affected by different GNNs? To answer this question, we propose Graph Distillation with Eigenbasis Matching (GDEM). Specifically, GDEM decomposes the graph structure into eigenvalues and eigenbasis. During distillation, GDEM matches the eigenbasis and node features of real and synthetic graphs, which equally preserves the information of different frequencies, thus addressing the spectrum bias. Additionally, a discrimination loss is jointly optimized to improve the performance of GDEM and balance its effectiveness and generalization. Upon completing the matching, GDEM leverages the real graph spectrum and synthetic eigenbasis to construct a complete synthetic graph, which prevents the spectrum from being affected by GNNs and ensures the uniqueness of the synthetic graph, thus avoiding the traversal requirement and improving the distillation efficiency.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'The contributions of our paper are as follows. (1) We systematically analyze the limitations of existing distillation methods, including spectrum bias and traversal requirement. (2) We propose GDEM, a novel graph distillation framework, which mitigates the dependence on GNNs by matching the eigenbasis instead of the entire graph structure. Additionally, it is theoretically demonstrated that GDEM preserves essential spectral similarity during distillation. (3) Extensive experiments on seven graph datasets validate the superiority of GDEM over state-of-the-art GD methods in terms of effectiveness, generalization, and efficiency.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'Before describing our framework in detail, we first introduce some notations and concepts used in this paper. ──────────────────────────────
1. OVERVIEW OF THE APPROACH

• Goal: Distill a small synthetic graph G′ = (V′, E′, X′) that, when used to train a GNN, yields performance comparable to a GNN trained on the real graph G = (V, E, X).  
• Key idea: Rather than matching gradients or representations (which are linked to specific GNNs and can cause spectral bias), GDEM directly matches the eigenbasis (a subset of eigenvectors corresponding to the smallest and largest eigenvalues) and node features of the real and synthetic graphs. It then “replicates” the real spectrum to reconstruct the synthetic graph’s Laplacian/adjacency structure.  
• Main components:
  - Eigenbasis Matching: Align a selected subset of eigenvectors U_K (from the real graph’s Laplacian) with a learnable synthetic eigenbasis U′_K.
  - Discrimination Constraint: Preserve category-level (class) information by matching representations (using a cosine-similarity loss) computed from the node features and spectrum.
  - Orthogonality Regularization: Ensure that the synthetic eigenbasis remains normalized and orthogonal.
  - Final graph reconstruction: Combine the learned synthetic eigenbasis with the replicated real spectrum to compute L′ and A′.

──────────────────────────────
2. PRELIMINARIES & DATA PREPROCESSING

A. Data Requirements  
  • Datasets: Use the seven benchmark graphs mentioned (e.g., Citeseer, Pubmed, Ogbn-arxiv, Flickr, Reddit for homophilic; Squirrel and Gamers for heterophilic).  
  • Data splits: For homophilic datasets, use the public splits; for heterophilic graphs, use the splits provided in the paper (e.g., training/validation/test ratios as specified).

B. Preprocessing Steps  
  1. Load the graph(s) (using libraries such as NetworkX or PyTorch Geometric) and extract:
     – Node features X ∈ ℝ^(N×d)
     – Adjacency matrix A (binary, symmetric, undirected)
     – Labels Y for the node classification task.
  2. Compute the normalized Laplacian L = I_N – D^(–½) A D^(–½) where D is the degree matrix.
  3. Perform eigen-decomposition of L:
     – Use a numerical solver (e.g., from scipy.sparse.linalg) to compute all or, preferably, a truncated set of eigenvalues λ_i and eigenvectors u_i.
  4. Choose a subset for matching:
     – Select K = K₁ + K₂ eigenvectors from the real graph: the K₁ smallest (low-frequency, capturing global structure) and the K₂ largest (high-frequency, capturing local details).  
     – The ratio rₖ (e.g., in {0.8, 0.85, 0.9, 0.95, 1.0}) sets K₁ = rₖ N′ and K₂ = (1 – rₖ) N′ where N′ is the number of synthetic nodes (dependent on the chosen compression ratio).

──────────────────────────────
3. SYNTHETIC GRAPH INITIALIZATION

A. Synthetic Node Features (X′)  
  • There are two options:
    – (Option 1) Random initialization or  
    – (Option 2) Pre-train an MLP on the real node features X (with labels Y) and then “transfer” this MLP to get an initial X′ for the synthetic graph.  
  • The paper notes that they “freeze” the well-trained MLP to fine-tune classification objectives on synthetic data.

B. Synthetic Eigenbasis (U′_K) Initialization  
  • Initialize a matrix U′_K ∈ ℝ^(N′×K) that will be optimized.  
  • One recommended initialization is by creating a synthetic adjacency matrix via a stochastic block model (SBM), then decompose it (e.g., using eigen-decomposition) to get an initial orthonormal U′_K.  
  • Alternatively, a random orthogonal initialization may suffice if SBM details are not available.

──────────────────────────────
4. MODEL LOSS FUNCTIONS & OPTIMIZATION

The overall objective is defined as a weighted sum:  
  L_total = α L_e + β L_d + γ L_o

A. Eigenbasis Matching Loss (L_e)  
  • Purpose: Make the synthetic eigenbasis U′_K “align” with the corresponding principal eigenbasis U_K from the real graph.  
  • Proposed formulation (subject to refinement based on your interpretation):  
    – For each selected index k, measure the difference between the projection matrices u_k u_k^T (real) and u′_k u′_k^T (synthetic).  
    – Loss term: L_e = ∑_(k=1)^K ||(u_k u_k^T) – (u′_k u′_k^T)||_F²  
  • Note: If a different formulation is suggested in Equation 3 (or similar) in the paper, adapt accordingly.

B. Discrimination Constraint Loss (L_d)  
  • Purpose: Preserve and match the category-level (class) information.  
  • How:  
    – Compute category-level representations H for the real graph (e.g., via X^T A Y or an aggregation over nodes by their class labels) and H′ for the synthetic graph.  
    – Apply a cosine similarity loss to ensure that H and H′ are aligned, thus enforcing that the synthetic graph “discriminates” between classes similarly to the real graph.
  • Loss term example: L_d = 1 – cosine_similarity(H, H′)

C. Orthogonality Regularization (L_o)  
  • Purpose: Maintain the normalized, orthogonal nature of the synthetic eigenbasis so that U′_K behaves like a proper eigenbasis.  
  • Formulation: L_o = ||U′_K^T U′_K – I_K||_F  
  • This prevents degenerate solutions when updating U′_K.

D. Optimization Protocol and Alternating Updates  
  • Training loop runs for T epochs (specific T may vary per dataset; consult hyperparameter table in the paper).
  • Alternating update schedule:  
    – Define τ₁ and τ₂ as the number of steps to alternate updates between the synthetic eigenbasis and node features.
    – For each iteration t, if t modulo (τ₁ + τ₂) is less than τ₁, update U′_K using learning rate η₁; else update X′ using learning rate η₂.
  • Use Adam as the optimizer for both sets of parameters.
  • Hyperparameters: α, β, γ; learning rates (lr_eigenvecs, lr_feat); update intervals τ₁, τ₂; number of epochs T.

──────────────────────────────
5. GRAPH RECONSTRUCTION

After the iterative optimization of U′_K and X′:

A. Replicating the Spectrum  
  • The method “directly replicates” the real graph spectrum, meaning that during synthetic graph construction you use the eigenvalues computed from the real graph.  
  • Reconstruct the synthetic Laplacian and/or adjacency matrix using:  
    – L′ = ∑_(k=1)^K λ_k (u′_k u′_k^T)  
    – A′ = ∑_(k=1)^K (1 – λ_k) (u′_k u′_k^T)

B. Output  
  • Save or return the synthetic graph: (A′, X′), where A′ represents the distilled structure and X′ the node features.
